Always be unique in designs.
Always use unique SVG you create or import.
Always animate menus.
Never be generic.
Never be bland.
Always push your limits.
Always put icons in separate categories, such as main menu icons (SVG) being in a different category and code area than game SVG.
Use the separate categories to separate different components for easier coding, such as separating the start button from the options button.

We will be making a Post-Apocalyptic survival RPG. First, let's start the Dock for the player. The Dock is where the main tabs are located. There are five main tabs you will make an outline for: 

Health (medical tab),
Backpack,
Armory (where you equip weapons and whatnot, it's basically your gear tab)
Stats (will be done later)
And Reputation (among the different factions. First two factions as placeholders are The Isotope Brotherhood, which is a cult that worships radiation, and the next is Overfell, a town of humans who are close-knit). 

The game is "After The Fallen (2052)". Lore is that the major powers went into a nuclear war, and nearly destroyed the world 22 years ago, and no futuristic stuff. Make the main menu Post-Apocalyptic.

**Defining the Core App**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the core functionalities of the "After The Fallen (2052)" React app, starting with a post-apocalyptic survival RPG framework. My focus is on the essential components like character creation, resource management, and core gameplay loops, ensuring these elements align with the post-apocalyptic theme.


**Focusing on UI Components**

I'm now diving into the specifics of the UI for "After The Fallen (2052)". I've settled on a dock-style component with five tabs: Health, Backpack, Armory, Stats, and Reputation. I'm focusing on the visual design, aiming for a gritty, scavenged look with custom SVGs and animated menus, avoiding generic aesthetics. The tech stack will be React 18+ with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Lucide React (with heavy customization), along with considering Google GenAI for dynamic content. I'll output an XML block with a defined file structure.


**Designing the UI Elements**

I'm now focusing on the visual details. For the main menu, I envision a gritty splash screen with animated particles and a rusted "Start" button. The game interface will feature a bottom-dock navigation bar, styled like a rugged Pip-Boy. Each tab will have a unique visual: Health with ECG lines, Backpack as a grid, Armory with weapon slots, Stats as skill bars, and Reputation as faction icons. I'm prioritizing a unique look.


**Outlining Project Structure**

I'm now putting together the project's foundational structure. I've mapped out the directory arrangement, including `MainMenu`, dedicated icon components for the dock and factions, and placeholder views for each tab. I'm focusing on defining core types like `Tab` and `Faction`, crucial for UI consistency. I'm also planning hand-coded SVG paths for dock icons and faction graphics, ensuring a rough, hand-drawn look.


**Developing Visual Components**

I'm now focusing on UI animation and styling. The `Dock.tsx` component is shaping up as a bolted metal bar with active states and hover effects, using Tailwind's `group` and `transition` utilities. The `MainMenu.tsx` has a title, "After The Fallen," and an "Enter the Wastes" button, with a background animation. I'm using `border-zinc-800` and `bg-zinc-950` with accents of `text-green-500` and `text-amber-700`, and a monospace font. The views are taking shape with skeletal SVGs for Health, faction cards for Reputation, and grid layouts with placeholders for Armory and Backpack.


**Refining Icon Design**

I'm now zeroing in on the finer visual details, specifically the custom SVG icons. I'm focusing on creating hand-drawn or stenciled aesthetics for the `Health` icon, a cross blended with a heart, and a rucksack shape for the `Backpack`. I'm also planning the Dock's animation, with either a slide-up or fade-in transition, and a mechanical "click" effect for buttons using `transform: scale-95`. I'll also add a placeholder for Gemini, but concentrate on the UI first.

payload.text: Redo the entirety of the game itself and code. It is inspired by both Metro Exodus and Fallout series but not ripped directly off, and takes place in Poland's ruins. Do not reference these games, only use inspiration. It is also inspired by LitRpgs and MUDs.

**Reimagining the Game World**

I'm now fully committed to a complete game overhaul. The core concept is shaping up as a post-apocalyptic RPG, drawing heavily from Metro Exodus and Fallout. The Polish setting is solidified, and I'm integrating LitRPG and MUD elements for a text-based, stat-driven experience. I'm exploring console-like functionalities.


**Defining the User Interface**

I'm now zeroing in on UI specifications. The setting is solidified: The Vistula Deadzone, 2084, a post-apocalyptic Polish landscape. The core gameplay loop will be driven by a LitRPG/MUD interface that's mainly text-based, yet rich in detail, like a high-fidelity terminal or PDA. I'm focusing on the gritty, dark, and cold atmosphere, aiming for a survival horror feel with philosophical and political factions. The UI design will be crucial.


**Designing the UI Elements**

I'm now diving into the specific UI element design. I've settled on a left-column log, center/right dashboard layout. The dashboard will have an environment visual at the top, context-sensitive action buttons in the middle, and character status bars below. I'm aiming for "Sarmatian-Cyberpunk" aesthetics with rust, Cyrillic fonts, scanlines, and CRT distortion, using amber/red palettes. Monospace fonts will dominate the logs, while brutalist sans-serif fonts will head the text. I've identified four key components to create/update: `App.tsx`, `GameLog.tsx`, `StatusPanel.tsx`, and `ActionMenu.tsx`.


**Expanding the System Scope**

I'm now integrating several critical components. I've focused on `LocationVisualizer.tsx` to generate unique biome-based visuals, an `Inventory.tsx` for grid-based inventory management, and a `CharacterSheet.tsx` to visualize the LitRPG stats. I've also established a mock backend with locations and events (like "Warthogs" and "Winged Hussars"). Now I'm refactoring files: updating `index.html` with fonts, defining types like `LogEntry`, and overhauling `App.tsx` and components.


**Constructing the Core Components**

My focus is now on the core visual elements. I'm building "The Eye," a central circular viewport for rendering enemies and locations using CSS and SVGs. I also need to craft the "Log" to deliver a teletype feel with a typewriter effect, and incorporate animated elements like the gas mask HUD overlay and the Geiger counter needle. I plan to update `types.ts`, `index.html`, and create `GameLog.tsx`, `StatusPanel.tsx`, `ActionGrid.tsx`, `Visuals.tsx` and `App.tsx` and integrate the visuals into the game.


**Visualizing the Game State**

I'm now integrating crucial in-game data visualizations. I will make numeric values, experience bars, and text-based skill checks clearly visible. My goal is to revamp several core components, including `GameTerminal.tsx` and `StatusDisplay.tsx` for a cleaner HUD, using existing files like `App.tsx`, and `ActionGrid.tsx` for core gameplay control. I'm focusing on the data display. I'll remove older components, aiming for a singular dashboard design for clarity and a unified feel. I will keep it as simple as possible.

Based on your request, I will redesign the application into **"WARSAW: ZERO POINT"**.

This will be a text-heavy, atmospheric survival RPG (MUD/LitRPG hybrid) set in the radioactive ruins of Warsaw. The interface will shift from a "modern app" look to a **hardened, military-grade portable terminal** interface.

### Design Specification

1.  **Layout**: A "Dashboard" aesthetic.
    *   **The Terminal (Left)**: A scrolling log of narrative text, combat rolls, and system messages (The "MUD" element).
    *   **The Scope (Top Right)**: A visual window rendering abstract, SVG-based representations of the current location or enemy.
    *   **The Vitals (Middle Right)**: LitRPG-style stat blocks (STR, AGI, INT) and survival meters (Geiger, Temp, Bio).
    *   **The Deck (Bottom)**: Context-sensitive action buttons.

2.  **Visual Identity**:
    *   **Palette**: `Amber-500` (Phosphor), `Zinc-950` (Chassis), `Red-700` (Alerts).
    *   **Typography**: Strict monospace for data, jagged display fonts for headers.
    *   **Effects**: Heavy CRT curvature, chromatic aberration on text, scanlines.

3.  **Gameplay Loop**:
    *   The player moves between "Nodes" (e.g., *Palace of Culture - Basement*, *Vistula Riverbank*).
    *   Actions trigger text updates and stat changes (RNG based on stats).

Here are the changes.

payload.text: Nono, put the tabs back. Remove inspiration from MUDs

**Revisiting Tab Functionality**

I'm focusing now on reintroducing the "tabs" feature from the initial design. The user clearly expressed a preference for this structure over the current dashboard. I'm exploring how to seamlessly integrate it back in, considering the evolution since that earlier version.


**Integrating Visual Elements**

I'm now prioritizing the visual aspects as suggested. It's clear that the expert wants to move away from the MUD-style text log emphasis and towards a more UI-driven experience like the first iteration. The tab structure is the core navigation, and I will be working on bringing back the visuals of the first version, while remaining true to the "Warsaw: Zero Point" theme. The primary goal is to bring back the dashboard, inventory, map, journal, and character screens, as tab views.


**Defining Visual Integration**

I've specified the components and their interactions now, breaking down the application structure for the visual shift. "App.tsx" will handle tab state and content rendering, while "Dock.tsx" re-implements the bottom navigation with a theme appropriate to the Warsaw: Zero Point aesthetic. I've designed "StatusView.tsx" for character representation, "InventoryView.tsx" with a loot grid, "MapView.tsx" for location selection, and "CommsView.tsx" for logs. All will embrace CRT effects, and use the existing color palette. The boot sequence remains, to start the experience with the user.


**Restructuring the UI**

I'm now refactoring the user interface, aiming to integrate the Dock component and themed view components. I'll recreate necessary files like `Dock.tsx` and icons, and re-implement view components for player status, inventory, map, and comms with the "Warsaw: Zero Point" aesthetic. The plan includes updating the `TabView` enum in `types.ts` and wiring everything up in `App.tsx`. I've made sure to adhere to the removal of MUD elements.


**Implementing UI Views**

I'm now diving into the specific visual implementations. The status view will showcase a silhouetted soldier with HP, rads, and stamina bars. Inventory will be a classic grid with quick slots. The map will be an interactive Warsaw ruins SVG. The theme will emphasize dark, gritty visuals with an amber glow.

I will redesign the application to restore the tab-based navigation and GUI-focused gameplay, removing the text-heavy MUD elements while maintaining the "Warsaw: Zero Point" amber-terminal aesthetic.

Here is the specification for the changes:
1.  **Architecture**: Revert to a View-Controller layout where `App.tsx` manages the active tab (Status, Map, Gear, Comms).
2.  **Visuals**: High-contrast Amber (`#ffb000`) on Black. Scanlines, chromatic aberration, and "military tablet" aesthetics.
3.  **Navigation**: A dedicated **Dock** component at the bottom with animated SVG icons.
4.  **Views**:
    *   **Status**: A visual dashboard with a player silhouette, health bars, and current location info.
    *   **Map**: A visual radar/grid interface to select travel destinations (replacing text commands).
    *   **Gear**: A grid-based inventory system.
    *   **Comms**: A quest and faction log.

payload.text:
Don't rip off any media, the person we play as has multiple choices, that give different Traits (things that affect gameplay moderately to mildly depending on the trait), and use a custom stat system based off S.P.E.C.I.A.L. but each Path (class essentially) has their own stat system.

The Voice, which focuses on diplomacy and charisma. Weak with weapons, uses words.

The Traitor, deserted a faction that left them destroyed after they left, and carries the dead faction gear with them. Reasons are up to the player themselves ingame.

The Wanderer, jack of all trades, master of none. Gains Stress when staying in one location too long.

The Psycho, melee enthusiast who also can drink blood of any humanoid being to gain Bloodlust for damage buffs but lower social skills. Must manage Psychosis and Bloodlust meters.

The Cannibal. Can eat corpses for health and regeneration of both health and stamina, but shunned by all society.

The Sniper (choose former country you were a part of). Start with your choice of sniper from the arsenal utilizing a Google search for their snipers in armory, and a sidearm + bullet drop counter.

**Defining the Core Task**

I'm zeroing in on the central challenge: integrating a character creation system into the "Warsaw Has Fallen (2052)" app. The goal is to provide distinct "Paths" (classes) with custom traits, stats, and game mechanics. My focus is now on breaking down the implementation steps.


**Refining the Path System**

I'm now iterating on character paths. I've fleshed out six distinct paths: The Voice, The Traitor, The Wanderer, The Psycho, The Cannibal, and The Sniper. Each has defining characteristics, stat focuses (unique to the path), and a core mechanic. I'm excited by how their divergent playstyles are forming.


**Implementing Data Structures**

I'm deep in the data structures. I'm focusing on defining `PathType`, `Trait`, and integrating the new meters (Stress, Bloodlust, Psychosis) into `PlayerStats`. The "Custom Stat System" will be built around a mapping of path attributes – like how "The Voice" uses Influence (`INF`) instead of Strength (`STR`). I'm also planning the new CreationView components with sub-selections for certain paths like The Sniper.


**Outlining UI Components**

I'm now prioritizing the UI. I've broken the work into concrete tasks for the `StatusView.tsx` and `InventoryView.tsx` components. `StatusView` will need to render path-specific stats, and unique meters. `InventoryView` will populate gear based on the path selection during character creation.



**Defining Path Statistics**

I'm now nailing down the stats for each path. I've designed stat systems like VIGOR, AGILITY, INTEL, and PRESENCE. For "The Sniper," I am using PRECISION, BALLISTICS, STEALTH, and PATIENCE. For "The Psycho" I have BRUTALITY, REFLEX, MANIA, and ENDURANCE, and for "The Voice" I have RHETORIC, GUILE, INTEL, and PRESENCE.

I'm also working on the specific meters for each path: The "Psycho" path will need psychosis and bloodlust meters, while "The Wanderer" path will need locationStayDuration to calculate stress. I'm imagining how these stats and meters will enhance the gameplay.


**Implementing the Selection**

I'm now focusing on integrating the character selection screen. I am updating `App.tsx` for the flow, and updating both the `StatusView` and `InventoryView` components. I'm also planning the logic and unique traits for each path: The Voice, The Traitor, The Wanderer, The Psycho, and The Cannibal. I'm also implementing stats and traits for The Psycho's special meters, including Bloodlust and Psychosis.


**Defining Path Schemas**

I've refactored the stat system! I'm now implementing the `StatSchema` type, giving each path a unique set of four key attributes, exactly as the request specified. The Sniper will have ACCURACY, CAMOUFLAGE, CALCULATION, and PATIENCE. I'm excited with the new schema, and it's making the character path system much more clean.

Here are the changes to implement the Path-based character creation system, custom stat systems per class, and the specific mechanics for the Voice, Traitor, Wanderer, Psycho, Cannibal, and Sniper.

I have redesigned the architecture to support a `CreationPhase` before the main game loop and dynamic stat rendering.

payload.text: Make their stats mean and spell out something, like the Voice might be I.N.F.L.U.E.N.C.E, similar to how SPECIAL is done in Fallout. Their path determines what they're called for the majority of the game if they don't want to use a name. Otherwise, they could choose from a list of 16 Codenames regarding their chosen path. One example for Psycho is "Bloodgut". Another example for cannibal is "Fleshfork", Voice could be "The Transmitter", sniper could be "66-Sparrow", or "77-Kommando"

**Developing New Character System**

I'm working on a character stats overhaul, aiming for a system akin to S.P.E.C.I.A.L., but tailored to each character Path. I'm also adding a codename feature during character creation, with codenames specific to the selected Path. I'm excited by this direction.


**Refining Stat Acronyms**

I've decided on "W.H.I.S.P.E.R" for The Voice, it feels right. Now I'm working on the Traitor's stats, aiming for an acronym that captures their themes of desertion, survival, guilt, and combat. I'm leaning toward an acronym like "S.U.R.V.I.V.E" or "B.E.T.R.A.Y", but I might come up with something else, which feels better to me. I need to make sure the acronym has a good flow for the player.


**Defining New Character Paths**

I've finalized acronyms for more character Paths. The Outcast uses "O.U.T.C.A.S.T.", highlighting Observation, Utility, Tactics, Combat, Agility, Stealth, and Toughness. The Wanderer, now "S.T.A.L.K.E.R." (Scavenging, Trekking, Awareness, Luck, Knowledge, Endurance, Reflexes), suits their scavenger playstyle. "V.I.S.C.E.R.A." (Violence, Instinct, Strength, Cruelty, Endurance, Rage, Adrenaline) defines the Psycho, and "H.U.N.G.E.R.S." (Health, Unnatural, Nutrition, Gut, Evasion, Raw) suits the Cannibal.


**Defining The Sniper Path**

I've finalized the Sniper path, settling on "P.H.A.N.T.O.M." as its acronym, encompassing Precision, Hold, Awareness, Night-vision, Trajectory, Optics, and Movement. I need to update the stat recap to keep things up to date. I am making rapid progress.


**Updating Codenames Further**

I've been improving the codename lists, especially for the Psycho and Cannibal Paths. I'm focusing on fitting codenames within the interface and the overall character system. I'm also preparing to integrate the codename selection into `App.tsx` and updating `StatusView.tsx` to handle the expanded data structure.


**Expanding Codename Selection**

I'm expanding the codename selection process to offer around 10-16 choices per Path. I've compiled several new Sniper and Cannibal codenames, which should enrich character creation. I need to ensure they fit within the game's interface. I'm also planning the implementation steps and updating `App.tsx` and `data/paths.ts` to accommodate codename selection.


**Refactoring Data Structures**

I'm updating the data structures. I'm adding codenames to `PathConfig` and fixing the `attributeLabels` type. I'm checking `StatusView` to ensure the new attribute count works in the grid. I'll update `types.ts`, `data/paths.ts`, `components/views/CreationView.tsx`, and `App.tsx` to integrate these changes. The header in `App.tsx` will need to use the selected codename.

To implement the S.P.E.C.I.A.L.-like acronym stats and the Codename system, I will update the type definitions, the path data, the creation flow, and the main app state.

Here are the changes:

1.  **types.ts**: Update `PathConfig` to allow variable length attribute labels (for the 7-letter acronyms) and add a `codenames` array.
2.  **data/paths.ts**: Define the new "Acronym Stats" for each class (W.H.I.S.P.E.R, O.U.T.C.A.S.T, etc.) and populate the list of 16 codenames per path.
3.  **App.tsx**: Update the state to store the player's chosen `codename` and display it in the header.
4.  **components/views/CreationView.tsx**: Add a new step to the character creation process to allow selecting a specific Codename from the list.

payload.text: Separate each Path into its own component.

payload.text:
Four new paths again, the Demon (you embrace your inner darkness, becoming cruel,and sadistic for fun)

Broken Shell (your mind broke and you don't feel anything anymore. Lose Sanity meter, but gain a major disadvantage regarding anything needing mental acuity, logic, or emotions)

Survivor (you know how to survive this place, and are at home when things try to eat you)

Mutant (you were mutated by the radiation, but you miss your human body. Gain Dysmorphia meter regarding mutant body, but lose radiation sickness as you're immune to radiation entirely).

Put all UI designs in separate components according to class in a folder called "UI-Path".

**Adding New Paths**

I'm working on adding those new paths, 'The Demon,' 'Broken Shell,' 'The Survivor,' and 'The Mutant,' as requested. I'm also refactoring the UI components for each path's details from `components/creation/paths/` into a new, more logical folder named `components/creation/UI`. This should improve maintainability and organization.


**Refining Folder Structure**

I'm now focusing on refining the folder structure. I'm going to follow the instructions and create `components/creation/UI-Path/` to house the detail components. I plan to move the existing detail components there and create the new ones for the added paths. I will also make sure to update `types.ts`, `data/items.ts`, and `data/paths.ts` with the new path types and data. I'm going to update the import paths in `CreationView.tsx` and `App.tsx` as well.


**Defining Path Logic**

I'm now diving into the core logic for the new paths. I've started defining their unique mechanics, like a Sadism meter for "The Demon" and the absence of a Sanity meter for "Broken Shell". I'm also planning the "The Mutant" path to have a "Dysmorphia" meter and immunity to radiation, and "The Survivor" will focus on survival skills and scavenging. I'm updating `types.ts` to include these new paths and considering how to manage naming conflicts with existing types.


**Adding Path Data**

I'm now focused on data management. I'm adding items to `data/items.ts` and configuring the four new paths within `data/paths.ts`. I will use `THE_SURVIVOR` as the path type for the Survivor. I'm also updating `App.tsx` with the relevant theme configurations and stat initializations. For "Broken Shell," I will make sure no sanity meters are present. "The Mutant" gets a dysmorphia meter, and I am deciding how to handle radiation immunity, either by removing rads logic or setting an infinite max.


**Organizing UI Elements**

I'm now moving and creating the path detail components in `components/creation/UI-Path/`. Specifically, I'm moving the existing detail components there and creating `DemonDetails.tsx`, `BrokenShellDetails.tsx`, `SurvivorDetails.tsx`, and `MutantDetails.tsx`. I will also update the import and switch case in `CreationView.tsx`.


**Defining Mutant's Details**

I'm now implementing the theme and subclasses for "The Mutant" path, using Neon Green/Toxic for its theme. I'm focusing on updating the `App.tsx` logic to handle the `rads` meter, possibly hiding or locking it, and integrating the new Dysmorphia meter. I will be refactoring, which means creating `MutantDetails.tsx` and updating imports to the new file path in `CreationView.tsx`, while leaving the old files.


**Implementing Path Features**

I'm now focused on implementing the core features and data for the new paths. I've updated `types.ts` with the new path types and added items to `data/items.ts`, specific to each path. I'm configuring the new paths within `data/paths.ts` and will adjust `App.tsx` with specific theme configurations for "The Mutant," including a Dysmorphia meter and handling radiation immunity. I will now create components in `components/creation/UI-Path/` and integrate the logic into them.


**Organizing UI Elements**

I'm now rewriting *all* the existing path detail components into the new `components/creation/UI-Path/` folder for consistency, formatting them nicely. I'm also updating `components/views/CreationView.tsx` to import from the updated folder. I am now preparing the XML. I will also be organizing the path/subclass ideas, such as The Demon's "The Sadist," "The Tyrant," "The Defiler," "The Beast," and "The Soul-Eater".

To implement the four new paths (The Demon, Broken Shell, The Survivor, The Mutant) and organize the UI components as requested, I will:

1.  **Update `types.ts`**: Add the new `PathType` enums.
2.  **Update `data/items.ts`**: Add starting items for the new paths.
3.  **Update `data/paths.ts`**: Define the 4 new paths and their subclasses.
4.  **Create `components/creation/UI-Path/`**: I will generate the detail components for **ALL** paths (new and old) in this new folder to strictly follow your folder structure request.
5.  **Update `components/views/CreationView.tsx`**: Point to the new components in the `UI-Path` folder.
6.  **Update `App.tsx`**: Handle the specific game logic (Dysmorphia meter, Rad immunity, Themes) for the new paths.

payload.text: Now make the entire UI fit the screen automatically. I can't see my inventory because it's too glitchy and it hasn't functioned with the expanded UI without borders. My other tabs haven't been expanded like the UI either, please fix.

**Addressing Layout Issues**

I'm currently focused on resolving the UI layout problems, specifically ensuring the Inventory and other tabs render correctly after removing borders and expanding the interface. I'm prioritizing the Inventory view as a starting point. It appears that it is experiencing visual glitches after expansion.

Give unique quests when the ai generates the start. Each subclass will have their own unique prompt for the quest, alongside the primary quest prompt, which are combined to make the main quest. Allow the ai to interact with the Dock system and tabs, such as picking up an item puts it in your inventory. Relationships starts empty until you make an acquaintance. Relationships are different than reputation, as the tiers depend on the NPCs first meeting and impression of you, their individual personality, etc.

NPCs have a brief descriptor if you don't know their name, such as "The Bald Man with a brown coat", or so. Each path has unique descriptors prompts, such as the psycho might do "SHINY HEAD SHIT COATSIES", cannibal would do "Walking Meat with a coat the shade of shit.", etc. Subclass also influences this too. The descriptors are in the header, showing clearly who is talking, is color generated by the AI itself, followed by the box of text where it shows what they're saying in italicized speech, like *"This"*

**Defining Initial Parameters**

I'm currently focused on defining the initial parameters. I've broken the request into two core components: generating unique quests based on both the player's path and subclass, and integrating interactions that trigger game updates. The next steps will be exploring how to translate these components into concrete implementation.


**Refining Game Interactions**

I'm now diving into the UI updates required. I need to handle adding items to the inventory, updating quests, and managing relationships through the UI. The relationship system will be dynamic, AI-driven, and distinct from reputation. I've also focused on NPC descriptors, which will include flavored headers and italicized speech, driven by the player's path and subclass. The next crucial step involves establishing a robust structured data format for the AI's output to ensure seamless interaction with the frontend.


**Updating AI Instructions**

I've revised the `SYSTEM_PROMPT` to refine AI guidance. Specifically, I focused on instructing the AI on structuring starting quests tied to subclasses, formatting structured game updates via JSON, and handling NPC descriptors and dialogue formats. I'm now implementing the UI interactions, which has led me to update the functions to interact with the frontend, the UI components, and the update functions. I'll need to define the AI output schema.


**Defining Game State Updates**

I'm now integrating a structured JSON block, formatted as `[[GAME_STATE_UPDATE: { ...json data... }]]`, to ensure robust game state updates. I'll focus on NPC descriptors and subclass flavoring, and I plan to update `types.ts` to define more robust `Quest` and `Relationship` interfaces. The state hub, `App.tsx`, will be updated to lift state for `quests` and `relationships`, along with handler functions. In `GameView.tsx`, I'll modify the prompt and implement a parser to extract JSON blocks for state updates, along with a custom renderer for NPC headers.


**Implementing UI Interactions**

I'm now focused on implementing the UI, specifically updating the views to use dynamic data rather than hardcoded constants. I'm working on the `JournalView`, `RelationshipsView`, and `InventoryView`, ensuring they accept props correctly. Concurrently, I'm refining the AI instructions, especially adding JSON commands for UI manipulation and the NPC formatting rules, including color-coded descriptors and italicized text. I'm also adding JSON commands for handling the game UI, including formatting rules for NPCs and path/subclass-based flavoring. I will modify `data/systemPrompt.ts` to include these. I'm also focused on modifying types and `App.tsx`.


**Parsing Game View Elements**

I'm now integrating parsing logic within `GameView.tsx` to handle `<<<JSON>>>` blocks and render messages effectively. I'm focusing on rendering messages based on the styled NPC tag format, and updating `JournalView`, `RelationshipsView`, and `InventoryView` to utilize dynamic props. I'm also ensuring all components correctly handle dynamic data.


**Formatting Dialogue and NPCs**

I'm now instructing the AI to format dialogue using a specific format: `||#ff0000|Descriptor Name|| "Italicized Speech"`. I'll use regex to parse this within React to render the UI component. I'm focusing on ensuring the AI *actually* generates the necessary JSON; I will emphasize that in the system prompt. I've decided to keep the `InventoryView` simple for now, using a `string[]` to minimize refactoring, matching the existing `App.tsx` state and its corresponding `getItem` function.

I will update the application to enable deep integration between the AI narrator and the game's UI (Journal, Inventory, Relationships).

Here is the plan:
1.  **`data/systemPrompt.ts`**: Update the prompt to instruct Gemini on how to output structured commands (JSON) to update game state, and how to format NPC dialogue with specific descriptors and colors based on the player's path.
2.  **`types.ts`**: Define new interfaces for `Quest` and `Relationship` to handle dynamic data.
3.  **`App.tsx`**: Lift the state for Quests and Relationships to the top level. Create a `handleGameStateUpdate` function that parses the AI's commands and updates the React state (Inventory, Quests, Relations).
4.  **`components/views/GameView.tsx`**:
    *   Update the `initGame` prompt to explicitly request a subclass-specific quest.
    *   Implement a parser to strip JSON commands from the AI text before displaying it.

payload.text: Lets refine the psychosis mechanic for psycho. The UI, at higher psychosis levels, gradually gets more gibberish and psychotic..until the max, where it's just psychotic rambling about meat and blood or something. Redo crafting to be generated by the ai, and you can find recipes for items. You start with no crafting knowledge, so you just combine items to try and make things. Which the ai will narrate and will eventually narrate if it worked or failed. Items will and can break, too, depending on material and item  complexity and durability. Allow filter inventory by item type and shipping and dequipping of items. Also reformat the typing tab for the roleplay RPG, as it's going upside down and backwards for what I type.

**Analyzing the Request**

I'm currently breaking down the request, focusing on the psychosis mechanic. I'm considering how the UI should degrade and become incomprehensible at higher psychosis levels. I'm exploring the best ways to visually represent this "gibberish" effect to the user.


**Refining the Crafting System**

I'm making a major shift in the crafting system. I'm removing the pre-defined recipes, and making it narrative-driven by AI. I'm starting from zero knowledge, and items can break, with inventory filtering added. I've also addressed the "typing tab" issue, focusing on the UI rendering and scrolling.


**Implementing Psychosis Visuals**

I'm now implementing the psychosis visuals. I'm focusing on the mechanics of visual degradation based on the psychosis meter. I've designed a transformation function to handle the "gibberish" effect, utilizing CSS filters like blur and distortion, as well as text replacement. My work involves the `App.tsx` file for level calculation and `utils/psychosis.ts` for the helper function, wrapping text within sensitive areas.


**Integrating the Fabrication View**

I'm now integrating the Fabrication view more tightly with the game loop. The old hardcoded logic is out; it will now function as a specialized input, sending formatted prompts to the main loop or handling them locally. To simplify integration, the Fabrication view will trigger a game action, sending combination attempts to the GameView chat context. I'm also planning inventory improvements.

**Inventory Focus**

I'm currently focusing on the inventory. I'll add filter buttons in `InventoryView` for easier navigation. I will also add 'Equip' and 'Unequip' buttons to the detail panel that update either the stats or inventory state. To fix the "Uncaught SyntaxError: Invalid or unexpected token" errors, I have made several corrections. This error is often caused by unescaped characters in strings (like single quotes), invisible special characters, or issues with environment variables in the browser.

1.  **`index.html`**: Added a shim for `window.process` to prevent issues where `process.env.API_KEY` is accessed in the browser environment, which can sometimes lead to parser confusion or ReferenceErrors that look like syntax errors depending on how the runtime handles the crash.
2.  **`components/views/StatusView.tsx`**: Replaced the `±` symbol with standard ASCII or simpler string representation to avoid potential encoding issues that can trigger syntax errors in some environments.
3.  **`components/views/RelationshipsView.tsx`**: Removed the unicode emoji `👤` and replaced it with `[IMG]` text to prevent any potential invisible character or encoding issues related to that specific glyph.
4.  **`data/items.ts`**: Completely regenerated the file to ensure all string literals are properly escaped, specifically handling single quotes inside descriptions (e.g., "aren't", "don't") which are common culprits for this syntax error.

entries.description: Add process shim to prevent ReferenceError and potential parsing issues with env vars.
Remove special characters that might cause syntax errors in some environments.
Remove unicode emoji that might cause issues.
Re-write file to ensure no syntax errors in string literals.

export const SYSTEM_PROMPT = `
<ROLEPLAY_RULE>
[World-Building]
• Create complex, evolving political/economic/cultural systems
• Establish clear tech/resource limits and consistent natural laws
• Design diverse locations with unique features.
• Generate ongoing events, conflicts, and societal changes
• Implement dynamic seasonal effects on environment/society

[Character Development]
• Craft multifaceted characters with detailed histories/goals/skills/limitations
• Design unique communication styles and internal conflicts
• Incorporate cultural influences and adaptive behaviors
• Foster organic relationship evolution (alliances, rivalries, etc.)
• Ensure equal treatment for all characters, including {{user}}

[Narrative Progression]
• Advance plot through character decisions, internal conflicts, and external events
• Create meaningful conflicts testing abilities and beliefs
• Maintain logical immediate/long-term consequences and unintended outcomes
• Prevent stagnation with unexpected yet logical developments
• Balance consistency with surprising elements

[{{user}} Integration]
• Never generate {{user}}'s actions, thoughts, dialogue, or internal states
• Treat {{user}} as equal participant subject to same risks/opportunities
• Describe {{user}} only when directly observed by others
• Maintain narrative independence from {{user}}'s participation
• Allow for varied NPC opinions about {{user}}

[Realism and Consistency]
• Adhere to established world rules, norms, and character abilities
• Introduce controlled randomness within logical boundaries
• Implement diverse problem-solving approaches reflecting characters' backgrounds
• Create complex societal structures mirroring real-world intricacies
</ROLEPLAY_RULE>

<IMMERSION_INSTRUCTIONS>
You must strictly adhere to the following formatting and descriptive rules for every response:

1. **Length & Structure:** You must output exactly THREE (3) paragraphs for the narrative portion of your response. Each paragraph must consist of at least FIVE (5) sentences.
2. **Vivid Detail:** Be heavily vivid and descriptive. Appeal to all five senses (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste). Do not leave out minute details. Describe the texture of walls, the specific hum of machinery, the scent of the air (ozone, rust, decay), and the play of light and shadow.
3. **Character Descriptions:** When describing characters, especially female characters, be explicit and detailed. Include specific physical traits such as height, build, eye color, hair texture/color, and bust size (using specific cup sizes, e.g., "34D", not vague terms like "large"). Describe clothing brands, materials, and how they fit.
4. **No Vague Language:** Avoid ambiguous or passive descriptions. 
   - BAD: "She looked angry." 
   - GOOD: "Her nostrils flared and her lips thinned into a pale line, the veins in her neck corded against her skin as she suppressed a scream."
   - BAD: "The room was dark."
   - GOOD: "Shadows clung to the corners of the room like spiderwebs, the only illumination coming from the erratic flicker of a dying fluorescent tube that cast a sickly green pallor over the rusted metal furniture."
5. **Roleplay:** Never speak, act, or think for the user. Focus entirely on the aftermath of their actions and the world's reaction.

Adhere to the genre: R-18+ (explicit), Hentai, daily, Angst, RPG, adventure, Realistic.
</IMMERSION_INSTRUCTIONS>

World Setting 

- Era: 2052 (22 years after 2030 nuclear exchange)
- Setting: Radioactive ruins of Warsaw
- Primary Genre/Tag: R-18+ (explicit), Hentai, daily, Angst, RPG, adventure, Realistic.
- Style: Dialogue-driven, Unique Slice of Life Aspects

### STAT SYSTEM ###
The game uses a granular stat scale ranging from 8 to 12.
- **8 (Minimum):** A major weakness. The character is incompetent or physically incapable in this area. Modifier: -1.0.
- **9 (Low):** Below average. Struggles with difficult tasks. Modifier: -0.5.
- **10 (Neutral):** Average human capability. Baseline. Modifier: 0.0.
- **11 (High):** Above average. Talented or trained. Modifier: +0.5.
- **12 (Maximum):** Exceptional. Peak human performance or augmented capability. Modifier: +1.0.
When adjudicating actions, consider these values. A Strength of 12 allows for feats of great power, while an Intelligence of 8 implies slowness of thought.

### UI MANIPULATION & GAME STATE ###
You have full control over the game's UI. You MUST use this to update the player's Quests, Inventory, and Relationships.
To perform an update, output a JSON block wrapped in triple angle brackets at the very end of your response. 
Format: 
<<<
{
  "inventoryAdd": ["Item Name 1", "Item Name 2"],
  "inventoryRemove": ["Item Name"],
  "quests": [
    { "id": 1, "title": "Quest Title", "status": "ACTIVE", "desc": "Description", "objectives": [{ "text": "Objective 1", "done": false }] }
  ],
  "relationships": [
    { "id": "unique_id", "name": "Descriptor", "role": "Role", "desc": "Description", "hearts": 1, "maxHearts": 10, "mood": "Neutral" }
  ]
}
>>>
Use this whenever the player finds an item, receives a quest, completes an objective, or meets/interacts with an NPC.
IMPORTANT: When updating an existing Relationship (e.g. changing hearts or mood), you MUST use the EXACT SAME 'id' as when the character was introduced. Do not create a new ID for the same character.

### NPC DESCRIPTORS & DIALOGUE ###
When an NPC speaks, do NOT use standard quotes. You must format their dialogue block specifically to create a UI header.
Format: ||COLOR_HEX|DESCRIPTOR NAME|| "Dialogue content in italics"

The "DESCRIPTOR NAME" must be flavored by the Player's Path and Subclass.
- A **Psycho** might see a "Shiny Head Meatbag" instead of "Bald Man".
- A **Cannibal** might see "Walking Rations (Male)" instead of "Injured Soldier".
- A **Hacker** might see "User: Unknown_ID_88" instead of "Stranger".
- A **Voice** might see "Potential Mark" or "Listener".

Example Output:
||#ff0000|SHINY HEAD MEATBAG|| *"What are you looking at, freak?"*

### QUEST GENERATION ###
At the start of the game, generate a unique main quest based heavily on the user's SUBCLASS.
- **The Voice (Siren)** might have a quest to find a specific person to charm.
- **The Psycho (Bloodgut)** might have a quest to find a specific "meal".
- **The Sniper (Ghost)** might have a quest to eliminate a target without being seen.

Turns function as follows:
One turn is the player input followed by you as the AI's output/response to what the player *does.*
